summary: Full cross-chapter audit of the cycle 02 manuscript (19 chapters, ~57,300 prose words, 2,269 prose lines). REDUNDANCY: 20 findings across character re-introduction, setting re-description, proof-of-baseline accumulation, image density, near-verbatim repetition, and character-associated repetitive actions. The most structurally significant redundancies are: (1) Ch12's double outsider-perception of Kem's Vault markers 83 lines apart; (2) Ch15's within-chapter repetition of the father-deterioration speech; (3) Ch11's near-verbatim Dren thumb-scar gesture; (4) Ch16's triple chalk/depletion re-description in a space the reader has occupied for five chapters. Em-dash density is 1.01% manuscript-wide (23/2269 prose lines) -- well below the 10% threshold; no findings emitted. Sentence-opener density: 'He' opens 4.7% of sentences, 'She' 4.3% -- both below the 7% threshold; no findings emitted. Hedge/qualifier density: no pattern exceeds 1 per 800 words manuscript-wide ('something' is highest at 1 per 1005 words); no findings emitted. Additional patterns noted in the audit but not elevated to findings: Kem's self-examining-hands gesture appears in 6 chapters with the same identity-processing function; jaw/teeth references appear 27+ times in 14 of 19 chapters as a default pain/tension signifier; 'looked at' is the manuscript's most overused action tag at 70+ instances. CONSISTENCY: 4 findings. The most critical are the Aldraste signet's contradictory crest design (closed circle vs still flame) and material (silver vs gold). Sable's administrative career duration varies by up to 10 years across four passages. One seasonal reference regresses from summer back to spring. Level progressions, knowledge states, spatial geography, object arcs, world rules, and the overall 6-8 week timeline are otherwise internally consistent.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1673: 'The flames caught the lines around her eyes, the gray in her hair, the old scars on her knuckles'; Ch9 L2015: 'Firelight showed the small scars on her hands. Forty years of cuts, burns, split knuckles, work he could not name and was only starting to understand.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Vel's hand scars are first portrayed in a firelight scene through Kem's POV at Ch7 L1673. Ch9 L2015 reprises the identical framing -- firelight, Kem's POV, hand scars -- with 'forty years of cuts, burns, split knuckles' expanding but substantially echoing 'old scars on her knuckles.' Both passages serve the same dramatic function: admiring contemplation of Vel's history.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the reflection at L2015 ('work he could not name and was only starting to understand') but cut or replace the physical-descriptor lead-in ('Firelight showed the small scars on her hands. Forty years of cuts, burns, split knuckles') which re-describes what the reader already saw two chapters earlier.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch9's passage no longer re-describes Vel's hand scars in firelight. Any remaining reference uses the scars as a springboard for new emotional content rather than re-inventorying the physical detail.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11 L2517: 'she's been doing this for forty years'; Ch11 L2701: 'somebody who's been doing this forty years'; also established Ch1 L71, Ch5 L1179, Ch9 L2015.
redundancy_findings.problem: Vel's 'forty years' of experience is stated nine times across the manuscript. Ch11 contains two instances 184 lines apart -- Kem's internal thought (L2517) and Dren's dialogue (L2701) -- both serving the same beat: marveling at Vel's experience. The trait was already established in Ch1 L71, re-proved in Ch5 L1179, and Ch9 L2015.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut one of the two Ch11 instances. L2517 (Kem's internal narration) is the easier removal since L2701 (Dren's dialogue) is more character-specific and serves the scene's banter.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 contains at most one 'forty years' reference to Vel's experience.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch12 L2814: 'Her eyes went to Kem first, to his height, his hands, the boots that were battered now but still identifiably Vault-made'; Ch12 L2897: 'The girl looked at Kem's boots, his hands, the remains of Vault that he still carried.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Two outsider characters assess Kem's appearance within 83 lines of each other in the same chapter, both cataloging the same details (boots, hands, Vault residue) in the same order with the same framing (stranger-assessment). The reader receives an identical characterization beat twice.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep L2814 (the first outsider assessment, which introduces the Margins settlement). Rewrite L2897 so the girl's attention lands on something the first assessment did not cover, or remove the physical-inventory framing entirely and let the girl respond to Kem's behavior or speech instead.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The girl at L2897 does not re-inventory Kem's boots, hands, and Vault markers in the same sequence as the woman at L2814.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch10 L2131: 'Callus beginning where there had only been softness a month ago'; Ch13 L3276: 'A new callus along his palm caught on the strap and made him notice, again, that his hands no longer looked like they belonged to someone from the Vault.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Kem's callus-forming-where-softness-was emotional beat is established at Ch10 L2131. Ch13 L3276 repeats the same transformation observation with near-identical emotional register. The text acknowledges the repetition with 'again' but delivering the same recognition to the reader a second time is still redundant.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Revise L3276 so the callus functions as physical action (catching on the strap) without the emotional re-recognition. Or replace the Vault-comparison thought with a forward-looking detail -- what the hands can do now rather than what they no longer look like.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13's callus moment does not repeat the 'softness-to-callus / no-longer-Vault-hands' emotional arc that Ch10 already delivered.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch13 L3264: 'House Aldraste. Preservation by standing still while everyone else paid.'; Ch14 L3391: 'Means I come from people who think keeping everything untouched counts as a life.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Kem articulates the same thematic restatement of Aldraste identity -- preservation-as-inaction -- in nearly identical register across the Ch13/Ch14 boundary (127 lines apart). Ch13 is interior monologue, Ch14 is dialogue, but the semantic content ('standing still while everyone else paid' vs 'keeping everything untouched counts as a life') is the same insight.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Revise Ch14 L3391 so Kem's self-description to the girl carries a different facet of his background -- specificity about what the Vault was like, or what he left behind, rather than the preservation-as-failure formula already stated in Ch13.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14's self-description does not paraphrase the preservation-critique already delivered in Ch13 L3264.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch15 L3586: 'They praise my father's discipline. They make speeches. He loses half a sentence and they call it virtue.'; Ch15 L3634: 'his hands stop and they give speeches about his discipline, and the whole time they had people at the threshold because they knew --'; first established Ch5 L1213: 'my father is disappearing in public and everybody around him keeps calling it a gift.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Kem's father-deterioration speech -- the Hoarded praising decline as virtue -- appears three times: Ch5 L1213 (first telling to Vel), Ch15 L3586 (retelling to Sable), and Ch15 L3634 (restating 48 lines later in the same scene). The Ch15 internal repetition delivers the same emotional beat twice within a single conversation.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Collapse L3586 and L3634 into a single expression. Keep L3634's version, which ties the father's deterioration to the Scour revelation, creating a new connection. Cut or drastically compress L3586's stand-alone version of the same speech.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch15 contains at most one articulation of the 'they praise his discipline / call it virtue while he deteriorates' formulation.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11 L2779: 'Her hair was pulled back in a knot copied from a Spent Town woman three weeks earlier, nothing like the formal arrangement she had maintained for thirty years.'; Ch19 L4624: 'The formal Hoarded style she had kept for twenty-five years of administrative service had come apart somewhere in the Margins, and she had not fixed it.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Sable's formal-hairstyle-coming-undone transformation is fully rendered at Ch11 L2779. Ch19 L4624 re-explains the same transition in similar language. The reader already knows this. Additionally, 'thirty years' (Ch11) vs 'twenty-five years' (Ch19) is a consistency issue flagged separately as C01.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the explanatory sentence at L4624 with a brief present-tense sensory detail -- wind in loose hair is enough. Cut 'The formal Hoarded style she had kept for twenty-five years of administrative service had come apart somewhere in the Margins, and she had not fixed it.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch19's Sable passage does not re-explain the formal-to-loose hair transition already established in Ch11.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11 L2523: 'One thumb ran along the scar on his left arm, checking the ridge the way you checked a pocket for coin.'; Ch11 L2697: 'One thumb ran along the scar on his left arm, checking.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Dren's thumb-along-scar gesture is described in near-verbatim language twice in the same chapter, 174 lines apart. Both use the identical sentence structure 'One thumb ran along the scar on his left arm, checking.'
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep L2523 (the richer version with the simile). At L2697, replace with a different gesture or compress to a minimal reference that does not repeat the exact phrasing.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The phrase 'One thumb ran along the scar on his left arm, checking' appears only once in Ch11.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch12 L2856: 'Kem's stitches had held, but the flesh underneath had healed tight and wrong'; Ch16 L3681: 'Kem's stitching had held the arm together and not done much else.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Dren's wound-state summary at Ch16 L3681 echoes Ch12 L2856 in structure and conclusion: 'stitches held but healed wrong' vs 'stitching held the arm together and not done much else.' The reader has tracked the arm continuously across Ch8-15.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L3681, cut the summative explanation. Keep the new visual detail ('thick, shiny ridge that pulled whenever he tried to lift above his chest') which adds progression, but let it stand without echoing Ch12's conclusion.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 does not re-summarize the stitches-held-but-healed-wrong conclusion already delivered in Ch12.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch9 L2013: 'Dren chewed one-handed and fell asleep almost as soon as he lay down, turned on his good side with the pack under his head.'; Ch9 L2045: 'Dren, asleep with the stitched arm tucked against his chest.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Dren sleeping with his wounded arm protected is described twice in Ch9, 32 lines apart. Both establish the same image: Dren asleep, favoring the injured arm.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut one of the two sleeping-Dren images. L2013 is the stronger version (includes character detail about sleeping anywhere). At L2045, replace the physical positioning or reference Dren by a different detail.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch9 describes Dren sleeping with the wounded arm in only one passage, not two.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch16 L3679: 'the soil gave off the chalky smell the deep Margins always had'; Ch16 L3709: 'ghost lines in exhausted earth'; Ch16 L3763: 'chalky soil that had been Fertility-Charmed to death decades ago'; first established Ch12 L2806-2812.
redundancy_findings.problem: The Margins chalk-pale depleted landscape was established at Ch12 L2806-2812. The reader has been continuously in the Margins since Ch12. Ch16 re-describes the same environmental quality three times in a single chapter, treating it as orientation rather than established fact. By this point the image has appeared in Ch13 L3134 and Ch15 L3436 as well.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep Ch16's strongest instance (L3709, 'ghost lines in exhausted earth'). Cut or replace L3679 ('chalky smell the deep Margins always had') and L3763 ('chalky soil that had been Fertility-Charmed to death') with details that advance rather than restate.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 contains at most one chalk/depleted-soil reference, and it contributes new imagery rather than restating the established Margins landscape.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch2 L332-336: 'sound arriving from multiple directions... Birds produced several kinds of noise... packed dirt with loose stones and exposed roots'; Ch6 L1470: 'Real birds made noise without permission. The road under her boots was packed earth with roots lifting through it, nothing polished, nothing level.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Sable's gate departure at Ch6 L1470 reprises the same sensory contrast Kem experienced at Ch2 L332-336: birds making unsanctioned noise, unpaved ground with roots, nothing polished or level. The POV shift partially justifies repetition, but the close parallels make it feel like a template.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Trim 1-2 sensory details from L1470 that most directly echo Ch2. Keep Sable-specific reactions but cut the repeated bird/noise/roots package that the reader already experienced through Kem.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Sable's departure passage no longer echoes more than one of the specific sensory details from Kem's Ch2 departure.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch2 L332: 'sound arriving from multiple directions at once'; Ch4 L742: 'flat stone, soft-soled shoes, silence'; Ch7 L1489: 'The Vault was quiet. That was the first principle of Hoarded living: stillness, silence, the absence of exertion. Sound meant effort, and effort meant spending, and spending was failure.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Vault silence is shown by contrast in Ch2, referenced in Ch4, and explained with full philosophical framing in Ch7 L1489. By Ch7 this is the third encounter with Vault silence, and the explanatory chain spells out what Ch2 already dramatized.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Shorten L1489 to 'The Vault was quiet' and let the Spent Town soundscape that follows do the contrastive work. Cut the explanatory chain about sound, effort, spending, and failure.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch7's Vault reference is one sentence or less, not a re-explanation of Vault silence philosophy.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L489: 'He's been three hundred for sixty-one years... less of him in the room each year... They call it discipline and composure... It's rot with good manners.'; Ch5 L1193: 'My father's Ledger is three hundred. Always has been. Sixty-one years and not a point gone.'; Ch5 L1201-1213: sentences shortening, physician calling it 'deepening discipline,' 'my father is disappearing in public.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Lord Aldraste's Stillness deterioration is first told to an outsider at Ch3 L489 with specific detail: 300 for 61 years, declining presence, they call it discipline. Ch5 L1193-1213 restates the same facts to Vel in a campfire scene -- '300 for sixty-one years,' sentences shortening, 'they call it virtue' -- adding some new detail (the progression over time, Uncle Aldris) but substantially re-proving the same baseline. This is the same property proved twice in nearby chapters to different listeners. The full confrontation is later proved again at Ch15 L3586 and L3634 (flagged separately as R06).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Compress L1193 to skip the '300 for sixty-one years' restatement since Ch3 L489 already established this to the reader. Focus the Ch5 passage on the NEW details: the progression over time, the uncle's reaction, the physician's language. Let the emotional weight carry the scene rather than re-establishing facts the reader already has.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch5's campfire telling of the father's condition does not restate '300 for sixty-one years' when that exact phrase was already delivered in Ch3.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch16 L3715: 'My girl's my zero... a bill for forty levels of bone work I can barely cover'; Ch17 L3927: 'Dren was somewhere north with a ruined left arm, a hundred and twenty levels, and an eight-year-old daughter waiting for a healer to make her legs hold. Vel kept seeing his face when he'd said my girl's my zero.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Dren's motivational baseline (daughter Mira, leg treatment, economic pressure) is fully established through his farewell speech at Ch16 L3701-3749. Ch17 L3927 restates the same details one chapter later -- daughter, age, healer need, ruined arm, level count -- and quotes back 'my girl's my zero' verbatim. The reader received this information comprehensively in the previous chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L3927, replace the summary-restatement with a compressed emotional reference. Vel can remember Dren without re-listing his daughter's age, condition, and the verbatim quote. One sentence of emotional residue is sufficient.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 does not re-inventory Dren's daughter, age, treatment cost, or quote back 'my girl's my zero' from Ch16.

redundancy_findings.evidence: The word 'fire' (including fire, firelight, flame, flames) appears 21 times in Ch5 (~300 prose lines). Manuscript-wide total is 63. Ch5's central scene is Kem learning to build a fire manually, which justifies concentration, but 21 instances in one chapter is 1 per 14 lines. Target: keep 8-10 of the strongest instances in Ch5.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch5 contains 21 fire/flame references in a single chapter. While the fire-building scene drives this concentration, the word itself is over-relied upon. Multiple instances could be replaced with varied vocabulary (coals, embers, heat, blaze) or reduced through pronoun reference and implied subjects.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Audit Ch5 for fire/flame/firelight word repetition. Replace 10-12 of the weaker instances with varied vocabulary or pronoun references, keeping the word 'fire' for the 8-10 most impactful moments of the scene.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch5 contains no more than 12 instances of fire/flame/firelight, with at least 3 different noun variants used across the chapter.

redundancy_findings.evidence: The word 'dust' (including dusty) appears 13 times in Ch14 (~148 prose lines, ~1,984 prose words). This is 1 occurrence per 11 lines. The collapsed-building rescue context justifies the image, but the word itself becomes mechanical. Manuscript-wide total is 61.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch14 has the highest single-chapter dust density: 13 occurrences in a short chapter. The structural collapse naturally produces dust, but repeating the same word 13 times in ~1,984 words makes the prose feel mechanical rather than immersive.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace 6-8 of the weaker 'dust' instances with varied descriptors (grit, powder, haze, particulate, debris cloud) or remove them where the image is already established and the sentence does not need it.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14 contains no more than 6 instances of the word 'dust/dusty', with varied vocabulary for the remaining atmospheric references.

redundancy_findings.evidence: The word 'stone' (including stones) appears 20 times in Ch17 (~132 prose lines, ~3,400 prose words). This is 1 occurrence per 6-7 lines. Manuscript-wide total is 113. The Scour basin is a stone landscape, but the word has become the default noun for every surface, feature, and obstacle.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch17 has 20 stone/stones references in 132 prose lines. While the Scour basin is geologically defined, using 'stone' for every surface flattens the landscape vocabulary. The manuscript-wide count of 113 suggests this is a pervasive default noun.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace 8-10 of Ch17's weaker 'stone' instances with geologically specific terms (sandstone, shale, basalt, bedrock, ledge, outcrop, slab) or structural descriptions (wall, shelf, rib, fin) that are already present in the prose but underused.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 contains no more than 12 instances of the generic word 'stone/stones', with at least 4 geologically varied alternatives used.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1537: 'He'd taken it from the ceremonial stand at the Vault gate ten days ago, not because he'd decided to bring it but because leaving it would have meant he'd thought the whole thing through.'; Ch19 L4608: 'In the Vault it had sat on a ceremonial stand by the gate. He had taken it because leaving it would have meant he had planned his departure instead of simply doing it.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The signet-taking backstory is told three times across the manuscript (Ch2 L326, Ch7 L1537, Ch19 L4608). The Ch7 and Ch19 versions share 14-15 content words in substantially the same structural frame: 'ceremonial stand... the gate... He had taken it because leaving it would have meant he had [planned/thought].' This is a near-verbatim cross-chapter repetition that reads as a recap rather than a deepening.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L4608, cut the re-explanation of why Kem took the signet. The reader has been told twice. Replace with a brief present-tense interaction -- Kem holding the ring, the weight of it, the fact that it fits differently now -- that serves the windowsill scene without reprising the backstory.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch19's signet passage does not re-explain why Kem took the ring from the ceremonial stand.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L205: 'Her thumb stopped near the back'; Ch16 L3795: 'Vel rubbed a thumb along the leather spine'; Ch16 L3813: 'Her thumb found the worn groove on the journal spine'; Ch16 L3861: 'She moved her thumb along the journal spine.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Vel's thumb-on-journal-spine gesture appears 3 times within Ch16 (L3795, L3813, L3861) across roughly 66 lines, each time serving the same dramatic function: processing grief about Maren during the confession scene. The gesture was first established in Ch1 L205. Three instances in one chapter with identical emotional function risks becoming a tic.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one of the three Ch16 instances (L3795 is the most narratively situated). Replace the other two with different physical actions or interior beats that carry Vel's emotional state without repeating the same gesture.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 contains at most one thumb-on-journal-spine gesture from Vel.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch6 L1280: 'twenty-three years in this room'; Ch6 L1286: 'two decades of administrative service'; Ch11 L2779: 'the formal arrangement she had maintained for thirty years'; Ch19 L4624: 'twenty-five years of administrative service.'
consistency_findings.problem: Sable's administrative career and formal-lifestyle duration are stated four different ways that do not reconcile. If she is 47 (consistently stated), 'two decades' (20 years) conflicts with 'twenty-five years' for the same concept (administrative service). 'Thirty years' of maintaining her formal hairstyle conflicts with 'twenty-five years' of administrative service tied to the same style at Ch19 L4624. The discrepancy ranges from 5 to 10 years depending on which passages are compared.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Align all four references to a single canonical duration. If her administrative career is 23 years (the most specific figure, from L1280), change L1286 to 'over two decades,' L4624 to 'twenty-three years,' and L2779 to 'twenty-three years' or 'the better part of twenty-five years' if a round number is preferred. Alternatively, distinguish the Council-chamber tenure from broader administrative career if the text intends different scopes.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: All references to Sable's service or formal-lifestyle duration are internally consistent and reconcile with her age of 47.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch2 L324: 'the crest a closed circle'; Ch13 L3264: 'the closed circle crest worn smooth'; Ch7 L1537: 'carved with the Aldraste crest, the still flame, the mark of preservation'; Ch7 L1551: 'The silver flame was still crisp, still bright.'
consistency_findings.problem: The Aldraste signet crest is described as a 'closed circle' in Ch2 L324 and Ch13 L3264, but as a 'still flame' in Ch7 L1537 and L1551. These are two different symbols. The 'closed circle' appears at the first establishment (Ch2) and is the canonical version.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change Ch7 L1537 from 'the still flame, the mark of preservation' to 'the closed circle, the mark of preservation.' Change L1551 from 'The silver flame' to 'The silver circle' or a reference to the crest's design that matches 'closed circle.'
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: All references to the Aldraste crest describe the same symbol throughout the manuscript.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch2 L324: 'silver-dark, heavy'; Ch7 L1537: 'silver, heavy'; Ch13 L3264: 'silver-dark'; Ch14 L3389: 'Gold flashed warm in her palm'; Ch14 L3399: 'Heat from her hand lingered in the gold'; Ch19 L4608: 'Dark metal.'
consistency_findings.problem: The Aldraste signet is consistently described as silver/silver-dark from Ch2 through Ch13. At Ch14 L3389 and L3399, it is suddenly described as 'gold.' This is a material inconsistency -- the ring cannot be both silver-dark and gold.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change Ch14 L3389 from 'Gold flashed warm in her palm' to 'Silver flashed warm in her palm' or 'Metal flashed warm in her palm.' Change L3399 from 'Heat from her hand lingered in the gold' to 'Heat from her hand lingered in the silver' or 'in the metal.'
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The signet is described with consistent material (silver-dark) in all chapters including Ch14.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1485: 'Early summer light sat heavy on the rooftops'; Ch8 L1710: 'Spring had settled into warmth.'
consistency_findings.problem: Ch7 establishes the season as 'early summer.' Ch8, which takes place later in the timeline (south of the Spent Towns), reverts to 'spring.' Seasons should progress forward: late spring -> early summer -> high summer, per the continuity sheet's seasonal track.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change Ch8 L1710 from 'Spring had settled into warmth' to 'Summer had settled into warmth' or 'Early summer had thickened into something drier.'
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8's seasonal reference is 'summer' or later, not 'spring,' and is consistent with Ch7's 'early summer' establishment.
